title: Fixing Invalid Git Cherry-Picked Commit Hash Validation

statement.markdown: Implement a function `validateCherryPickedCommitHash` that validates a given Git cherry-picked commit hash. The function should return `true` if the commit hash is valid (i.e., it matches the format of a standard Git commit hash, which consists of 40 hexadecimal characters) and `false` otherwise. You can assume that the input will be a string of characters. Note that the function should handle cases where the input is an empty string or contains non-hexadecimal characters.
statement.inputFormat: A string containing a Git cherry-picked commit hash
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the commit hash is valid
statement.notes: This function should be efficient and handle edge cases correctly.

hints.text: Use a regular expression to match the commit hash format
Think about how to handle edge cases like empty strings or non-hexadecimal characters

editorial.title: Editorial: Validating Git Cherry-Picked Commit Hashes
editorial.intuition: The key insight here is to use a regular expression to match the commit hash format.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force approach is to check each character of the string to see if it's a hexadecimal digit.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a regular expression to match the commit hash format.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a regular expression to match the commit hash format and handle edge cases correctly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The regular expression `^[0-9a-fA-F]{40}$` matches the commit hash format.
editorial.dryRun: Let's try a few test cases to see how the function works.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle edge cases correctly
